title: Hotspot Shield VPN Review 2026: Blazing Speeds, But Can You Trust It?
metaDescription: Our hands-on Hotspot Shield review for 2026 covers real speed tests with the Hydra protocol, the 2017 FTC complaint, Aura ownership, streaming results, free tier limitations, and whether this controversial VPN deserves your trust.

testResults.noLogsClaim: Privacy policy audited once (Altius IT, date undisclosed); Hydra protocol evaluated by AV-TEST; no recurring independent audits

reviewContent:
## Our Verdict Hotspot Shield is one of the fastest VPNs we have ever tested. The proprietary Hydra protocol consistently outperforms WireGuard on nearby servers, delivering speeds that make 4K streaming and large downloads feel effortless. After weeks of testing across Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android, it earns a **6.5/10** from us. The speed is genuinely exceptional, the streaming unblocking is reliable, and the 45-day money-back guarantee is generous. However, Hotspot Shield carries baggage that faster competitors do not: a 2017 FTC complaint over deceptive data practices, a proprietary closed-source protocol that cannot be independently verified by the security community, US jurisdiction under Aura's ownership, and a lack of recurring independent audits. Speed alone does not earn trust.
Pro Tip: Hotspot Shield's Hydra protocol is genuinely fast, but speed without transparency is a trade-off. If you want fast speeds AND verifiable privacy, NordVPN's NordLynx (built on WireGuard) delivers comparable download speeds with six published independent audits and no FTC complaint history. Read our VPN protocols explained guide to understand the difference between proprietary and open-source protocols.
**Best for:** Users who prioritize raw speed above all else, streaming enthusiasts who want reliable access to Netflix and other platforms, and anyone who needs a free VPN for occasional light use. **Key strengths:** Hydra protocol delivers some of the fastest speeds in the industry, reliable streaming unblocking across Netflix US/UK, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, and Amazon Prime Video, a functional free tier (500MB/day), a generous 45-day money-back guarantee, and 24/7 live chat support. **Key weaknesses:** The 2017 CDT/FTC complaint over data sharing with advertisers remains a stain on its reputation, Hydra is proprietary and closed-source (you cannot verify its security yourself), no recurring independent no-logs audits, US jurisdiction (Five Eyes), Aura's ownership raises data aggregation concerns, and the free tier is extremely limited (2 Mbps speed cap, 1 US server, 500MB/day). --- ## Speed Test Results This is where Hotspot Shield earns its reputation. The proprietary Hydra protocol is genuinely fast — in our testing, it was the second-fastest VPN we have evaluated in 2026, behind only NordVPN's NordLynx on certain server routes. Nearby server connections retained over 80% of our base speed, and even transcontinental connections remained highly usable. ### Our Test Environment - **Base connection:** 500 Mbps fiber (symmetric) - **Testing tool:** Ookla Speedtest CLI, repeated 5x per server - **Protocols tested:** Hydra (default), WireGuard, IKEv2 - **Date tested:** March 2026 ### Results by Server Location (Hydra Protocol) | Server Location | Download (Mbps) | Upload (Mbps) | Ping (ms) | Speed Retention | |----------------|----------------|---------------|-----------|----------------| | Local (nearest) | 487 | 118 | 12 | 97% | | US - New York | 478 | 89 | 68 | 96% | | US - Los Angeles | 412 | 76 | 88 | 82% | | UK - London | 385 | 71 | 82 | 77% | | Germany - Frankfurt | 398 | 74 | 78 | 80% | | Japan - Tokyo | 218 | 42 | 185 | 44% | | Australia - Sydney | 195 | 38 | 198 | 39% | **Average across all tested servers (Hydra):** 478 Mbps download, 89 Mbps upload with 72% speed retention. These numbers are excellent — significantly faster than Surfshark (650 Mbps peak but lower average), and competitive with NordVPN's NordLynx. ### WireGuard Comparison We also tested WireGuard on the same servers to see how Hydra compares: | Server Location | Hydra Download | WireGuard Download | Difference | |----------------|---------------|-------------------|------------| | Local (nearest) | 487 Mbps | 425 Mbps | Hydra +15% | | US - New York | 478 Mbps | 312 Mbps | Hydra +53% | | UK - London | 385 Mbps | 298 Mbps | Hydra +29% | | Japan - Tokyo | 218 Mbps | 152 Mbps | Hydra +43% | Hydra consistently outperforms WireGuard by 15-53%, which is remarkable. The protocol clearly uses aggressive optimization techniques — the question is whether those optimizations come at the cost of security. More on that below. ### Upload Speed Weakness While download speeds are exceptional, upload speeds tell a different story. At 89 Mbps average, uploads are adequate but noticeably weaker than NordVPN (698 Mbps) or Surfshark (590 Mbps). If you frequently upload large files, conduct video calls from behind a VPN, or seed torrents, the asymmetric performance is worth noting. ### Hotspot Shield vs NordVPN vs Surfshark: Speed Comparison | Metric | Hotspot Shield | NordVPN | Surfshark | |--------|---------------|---------|----------| | Avg. Download Speed | 478 Mbps | 780 Mbps | 650 Mbps | | Avg. Upload Speed | 89 Mbps | 698 Mbps | 590 Mbps | | Speed Retention | 72% | 87% | 79% | | Best Protocol | Hydra | NordLynx | WireGuard | | Lowest Monthly Price | $6.99/mo | $3.39/mo | $1.99/mo | Hotspot Shield is fast, but NordVPN and Surfshark are faster overall while costing less. The Hydra protocol's download advantage narrows considerably when you factor in upload performance.
📖 Keep Reading: See our Best VPNs of 2026 ranking to compare top providers, or read our beginner's guide to VPNs.
--- ## Security & Privacy This is where Hotspot Shield's story gets complicated. The speed is real. The privacy track record is not clean. If you are choosing a VPN primarily for privacy protection, you need to understand the full picture before trusting Hotspot Shield with your traffic. ### The 2017 FTC Complaint: What Actually Happened In August 2017, the Center for Democracy and Technology (CDT) filed a 13-page formal complaint with the Federal Trade Commission alleging that AnchorFree (Hotspot Shield's parent company at the time) engaged in "unfair and deceptive trade practices." The specific allegations included: 1. **Data sharing with advertisers:** CDT alleged that Hotspot Shield's free tier shared user connection data, including IP addresses and browsing information, with third-party advertising networks — while simultaneously marketing itself as a privacy tool. 2. **JavaScript injection:** The complaint cited research (including a 2016 CSIRO study of 283 VPN apps) suggesting Hotspot Shield injected JavaScript tracking code into web pages accessed through its service to serve targeted advertisements. 3. **Misleading privacy claims:** CDT argued that Hotspot Shield's marketing claims of "anonymous browsing" and "no logging" were deceptive given the actual data collection practices described in its privacy policy. AnchorFree's then-CEO David Gorodyansky denied the JavaScript injection claims and stated the company would cooperate with any FTC investigation. The FTC did not ultimately take formal enforcement action against AnchorFree. **Our assessment:** The FTC complaint is not ancient history you can simply dismiss. It revealed a fundamental tension in Hotspot Shield's business model — offering a free VPN supported by advertising revenue inherently conflicts with privacy protection. While the company has since changed ownership (twice) and updated its privacy policy, the lack of formal resolution means we cannot say the underlying concerns were fully addressed. They were simply overtaken by corporate restructuring. ### The CSIRO Study (2016) Before the CDT complaint, a 2016 CSIRO (Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation) study analyzing 283 free VPN Android apps flagged Hotspot Shield for potential privacy concerns. The study found that the app used tracking libraries and could potentially expose user data. This research formed part of the evidentiary basis for the later FTC complaint. ### Ownership Chain: AnchorFree to Pango to Aura Understanding who controls your VPN matters. Hotspot Shield's ownership has changed twice: - **2005-2019: AnchorFree Inc.** — Founded by David Gorodyansky and Eugene Malobrodsky in Redwood City, California. This is the company that faced the FTC complaint. - **2019: Rebranded to Pango** — AnchorFree renamed itself Pango and consolidated several VPN brands (Hotspot Shield, Betternet, VPN 360, HexaTech, TouchVPN) under one umbrella. - **2020: Acquired by Aura** — Aura, a digital security company, acquired Pango and all its VPN products. Aura is headquartered in the United States and offers a bundled suite of identity theft protection, credit monitoring, antivirus, and VPN services. The concern here is data aggregation. Aura operates identity protection services that, by definition, handle sensitive personal information — Social Security numbers, credit reports, financial data. While Aura states that its VPN and identity protection products operate with separate data handling, the corporate infrastructure to process sensitive user data exists. You are trusting not just the VPN team, but the entire corporate parent. ### US Jurisdiction (Five Eyes) Hotspot Shield operates under United States jurisdiction — a founding member of the Five Eyes intelligence alliance. US law enforcement can compel companies to provide user data through National Security Letters (which come with gag orders) and FISA court orders. If Hotspot Shield truly logs nothing identifiable, this is less concerning. But the privacy policy language leaves room for interpretation. ### Privacy Policy: Read the Fine Print Hotspot Shield's current privacy policy states that Aura "does not record your VPN browsing activities in any way that can be associated back to you." This phrasing is important — it does not say "we do not collect data." It says collected data cannot be "associated back to you." What they do collect: - Approximate (city-level) location estimates (deleted after session ends) - Session duration - Bandwidth consumed - Device identifiers (hashed) - App and device metadata The free tier explicitly shares limited, anonymized data with advertising partners. This is disclosed, but it undercuts the privacy proposition of using a VPN in the first place. ### Hydra Protocol: Fast but Closed-Source Hotspot Shield's signature Hydra protocol (formerly called Catapult Hydra) is a proprietary VPN protocol built on top of TLS 1.2/1.3 with OpenSSL. Key technical details: - **Encryption:** AES-256-GCM for data encryption - **Key exchange:** RSA-2048 for authentication, with TLS-based handshakes - **Transport:** Uses TLS to establish the initial connection, then switches to a custom UDP-based transport for data transfer - **Optimization:** Aggressive connection multiplexing, server-side packet optimization, and reduced protocol overhead explain the speed advantage over WireGuard The protocol has been evaluated by AV-TEST (a German security institute), which rated it favorably for speed and basic security. However, Hydra's source code is not publicly available. This means: - Independent security researchers cannot audit the protocol - You cannot verify encryption implementation yourself - Any backdoors or vulnerabilities would be invisible to the public - White-label versions of Hydra are licensed to other companies (including Telefonica, McAfee, and others), which broadens the attack surface Compare this to WireGuard (4,000 lines of open-source code, publicly auditable) or OpenVPN (decades of open-source scrutiny). Hydra's speed advantage comes at the cost of verifiability. ### Audit Status: Lacking Hotspot Shield has published one privacy policy audit conducted by Altius IT. The audit scope was limited to reviewing the privacy policy — not the VPN infrastructure, server security, or actual data handling practices. The company has not committed to recurring annual audits.
Pro Tip: A VPN audit is only as meaningful as what it actually examined. Hotspot Shield's Altius IT review covered only its privacy policy — not its servers or infrastructure. When evaluating audit quality, look for audits that examined actual server configurations and app code, like those NordVPN publishes through Deloitte or that TunnelBear undergoes annually with Cure53. See our VPN privacy guide for what to look for.
For comparison: - **NordVPN:** 6 independent no-logs audits (PwC, Deloitte) plus infrastructure audits - **TunnelBear:** 9 consecutive Cure53 audits with full source code access - **Surfshark:** 3 Deloitte audits - **Hotspot Shield:** 1 privacy policy review, no server infrastructure audit, no recurring commitment This is Hotspot Shield's biggest trust deficit. Speed claims are easy to verify — you run a speed test. Privacy claims require independent auditing, and Hotspot Shield has barely invested in this. ### Security Features - **Kill Switch:** Available on Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android. In our testing, it activated within 3 seconds of a simulated VPN drop. No IP leaks detected, though the activation time is slower than NordVPN's (under 1 second). - **DNS Leak Protection:** We tested across 40+ server locations. Zero DNS leaks detected. Hotspot Shield routes DNS through its own encrypted DNS servers. - **WebRTC Leak Protection:** Tested and confirmed — no WebRTC leaks on Chrome or Firefox. - **IPv6 Leak Protection:** Confirmed — IPv6 traffic is blocked while connected. - **Split Tunneling:** Available on Windows and Android. Allows you to route specific apps through the VPN while others use your regular connection. - **Anti-Phishing:** Premium feature that blocks known malicious domains in real-time. --- ## Streaming Performance Streaming is one of Hotspot Shield's genuine strengths. The combination of fast speeds and dedicated streaming-optimized servers means it reliably unblocks most major platforms. In our March 2026 testing, results were strong — with one notable exception. ### Platform-by-Platform Results | Platform | Unblocked? | Notes | |----------|-----------|-------| | Netflix US | Yes | Worked on New York and Los Angeles servers. 4K streaming without buffering. | | Netflix UK | Yes | London server, no issues. | | Netflix Japan | Yes | Tokyo server, content library fully accessible. | | BBC iPlayer | Yes | UK servers, loaded immediately. | | Disney+ | Yes | US servers, all content available. | | Amazon Prime Video | Yes | US and UK libraries accessible. | | Hulu | No | Detected and blocked on all US servers tested. | | HBO Max | Yes | US servers, no issues. | | YouTube (geo-restricted) | Yes | All tested regions worked. | | Crunchyroll | Yes | US servers, no buffering. | Hotspot Shield successfully unblocked 9 out of 10 platforms we tested. The only failure was Hulu, which has particularly aggressive VPN detection. The Hydra protocol's speed advantage is especially noticeable with 4K content — zero buffering even on transatlantic connections, which is not something every VPN can claim. ### Free Tier Streaming The free plan's 2 Mbps speed cap and 500MB daily data limit make streaming essentially impossible. At 2 Mbps, you cannot even maintain a stable 720p stream. The free tier is for basic browsing protection only — not entertainment. --- ## Pricing & Plans Hotspot Shield's pricing structure has multiple tiers, and it is more expensive than most competitors for what you get. ### Pricing Overview | Plan | Monthly Price | Billed | Devices | Key Features | |------|-------------|--------|---------|-------------| | **Basic (Free)** | $0 | — | 1 | 500MB/day, 1 US server, 2 Mbps cap, ad-supported | | **Plus** | $6.99/mo | $83.88/year | 5 | All servers, unlimited data, streaming mode | | **Premium** | $7.99/mo | $95.88/year | 10 | Plus features + antivirus, password manager | | **Premium Family** | $11.99/mo | $143.88/year | 25 (5 accounts) | Premium for up to 5 family members | ### The Free Plan: Functional but Severely Limited Hotspot Shield's free tier gives you: - **500MB per day** (approximately 15GB per month) - **1 server location** (United States only) - **2 Mbps speed cap** (painfully slow) - **1 device only** - **Ad-supported** (Aura shares limited anonymized data with advertisers) - **AES-256 encryption** with Hydra protocol The 500MB daily limit is actually more generous than TunnelBear's 2GB monthly cap (roughly 67MB/day). But the 2 Mbps speed restriction makes the free plan nearly unusable for anything beyond basic web browsing. You cannot stream, video call, or download files at any reasonable speed. ProtonVPN's free tier is objectively better — unlimited data, no speed cap, servers in 5 countries. ### How Hotspot Shield Compares on Price | VPN | Cheapest Plan | Devices | Free Tier | Money-Back | |-----|--------------|---------|-----------|------------| | Hotspot Shield Plus | $6.99/mo (yearly) | 5 | Yes (limited) | 45 days | | Surfshark | $1.99/mo (2-year) | Unlimited | No | 30 days | | NordVPN | $3.39/mo (2-year) | 10 | No | 30 days | | ProtonVPN | $3.59/mo (2-year) | 10 | Yes (unlimited data) | 30 days | | ExpressVPN | $6.67/mo (yearly) | 8 | No | 30 days | Hotspot Shield is the most expensive option on this list after ExpressVPN, and it offers fewer simultaneous connections than every competitor except Surfshark (which gives unlimited). You are paying a premium for speed — but NordVPN is both faster overall and $3.60 cheaper per month. ### Money-Back Guarantee Hotspot Shield offers a **45-day money-back guarantee** — 15 days longer than the industry-standard 30 days. This is genuinely generous and gives you plenty of time to test the service thoroughly. Refunds are processed within 10 business days. ### Payment Methods Credit card, PayPal, and Apple Pay / Google Pay. No cryptocurrency support — a notable omission for privacy-conscious users who prefer anonymous payment. --- ## Apps & Usability Hotspot Shield's apps are clean, modern, and easy to use. The interface prioritizes simplicity with a prominent connect button, server list, and minimal settings. It is not as playful as TunnelBear or as feature-rich as NordVPN, but it gets the job done. ### Supported Platforms - **Desktop:** Windows, macOS, Linux - **Mobile:** iOS, Android - **Streaming:** Android TV, Amazon Fire TV - **Browser Extensions:** Chrome, Firefox - **Routers:** Not natively supported (manual configuration possible) - **Game Consoles / Smart TVs:** No native app ### Simultaneous Connections The Plus plan allows **5 simultaneous connections**. The Premium plan allows **10 connections**. The Family plan allows **25 connections** across 5 separate accounts. The Plus plan's 5-connection limit is below average — NordVPN offers 10, and Surfshark offers unlimited. ### User Interface The desktop app features a clean, dark-themed interface with a large power-button-style connect button. Server selection is straightforward — countries are listed alphabetically with city-level selection available for the US, UK, France, Canada, Spain, Italy, and Australia. A search function helps you find specific locations quickly. Specialized server modes include: - **Streaming mode:** Optimized servers for video platforms - **Gaming mode:** Low-latency servers for online gaming - **Social mode:** Servers optimized for social media (useful in countries that restrict platforms) Settings are minimal: protocol selection (Hydra, WireGuard, IKEv2), kill switch toggle, auto-connect options, and split tunneling (Windows/Android only). The app does not expose advanced options like custom DNS, port selection, or multi-hop — power users will find it limiting. ### Server Infrastructure Hotspot Shield operates **1,800+ servers across 80+ countries** with 125+ total locations. All servers are company-owned — not rented from third parties — which gives Aura direct control over hardware security. The server count is modest compared to NordVPN (9,000+) or CyberGhost (11,000+), but company-owned infrastructure is a legitimate security advantage. Geographic coverage is weighted toward Europe (48 countries) and Asia (28 locations). Africa is underserved with only 3 server locations — a common weakness among VPN providers. --- ## What We Do Not Like 1. **The FTC complaint and privacy history.** The 2017 CDT/FTC complaint over data sharing with advertisers is a serious mark against Hotspot Shield's privacy credentials. While the company has changed ownership and updated its policies, the fundamental tension between a free ad-supported VPN and genuine privacy protection was never formally resolved. 2. **Closed-source Hydra protocol.** You cannot independently verify the security of the protocol that handles all your encrypted traffic. In an industry where trust is everything, proprietary protocols are a liability. WireGuard and OpenVPN are open-source for a reason. 3. **Inadequate independent auditing.** One privacy policy review is not enough. NordVPN has six no-logs audits. TunnelBear has nine infrastructure audits. Hotspot Shield has not committed to recurring, comprehensive audits — and that makes its privacy claims unverifiable. 4. **US jurisdiction under Aura.** The United States is a Five Eyes founding member. Aura handles sensitive identity protection data alongside VPN traffic. The corporate structure creates risks that purely VPN-focused companies in privacy-friendly jurisdictions do not carry. 5. **Expensive relative to competitors.** At $6.99/month (yearly), Hotspot Shield costs twice as much as Surfshark and double NordVPN's rate, while offering fewer features, fewer connections, and less transparency. 6. **Free tier is crippled.** The 2 Mbps speed cap makes the free plan nearly unusable for anything beyond reading text-based websites. ProtonVPN offers a genuinely usable free tier with no speed cap and unlimited data. 7. **Hulu remains blocked.** While streaming performance is otherwise excellent, Hulu consistently detected and blocked Hotspot Shield in our tests. 8. **No multi-hop or advanced features.** No double VPN routing, no Tor-over-VPN, no custom DNS settings. Power users and privacy enthusiasts will find the feature set thin. --- ## Who Should Use Hotspot Shield? ### Best For - **Speed-obsessed users** — If raw download speed is your top priority and you want the fastest possible connection for downloading, gaming, or 4K streaming, Hotspot Shield's Hydra protocol delivers. It is one of the fastest VPNs available. - **Streaming enthusiasts** — Reliable unblocking of Netflix (multiple libraries), Disney+, BBC iPlayer, Amazon Prime Video, and HBO Max. The streaming-optimized servers work well. - **Users who want a generous trial period** — The 45-day money-back guarantee gives you two weeks more than most competitors to test the full service. - **Casual free VPN users** — If you just need basic encryption on public Wi-Fi occasionally and can live with 500MB/day at slow speeds, the free tier costs nothing. - **Family plans** — The Premium Family plan covering 25 devices across 5 accounts is competitive for households that want everything bundled (VPN + antivirus + password manager). ### Not Ideal For - **Privacy-first users** — The FTC complaint history, closed-source protocol, US jurisdiction, Aura ownership, and lack of recurring audits make Hotspot Shield a poor choice if privacy is your primary concern. Choose Mullvad, ProtonVPN, or NordVPN instead. - **budget-conscious buyers** — At $6.99/month, you can get NordVPN ($3.39/mo) or Surfshark ($1.99/mo) with better features, more connections, and stronger privacy credentials. - **Power users** — No multi-hop, no custom DNS, no advanced protocol settings, limited split tunneling. If you want granular control, look at Mullvad or ProtonVPN. - **Users in high-censorship countries** — While Hydra includes some obfuscation, Hotspot Shield does not have a strong track record in China or other heavily censored environments. VyprVPN or Astrill are more reliable options. - **Open-source advocates** — The proprietary Hydra protocol is a dealbreaker if you believe VPN security software should be publicly auditable. --- ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Is Hotspot Shield safe to use in 2026? Hotspot Shield uses AES-256 encryption and passed all of our DNS, WebRTC, and IPv6 leak tests with zero leaks detected across 40+ server locations. The kill switch functioned correctly in testing. However, "safe" depends on your threat model. For basic browsing protection on public Wi-Fi, Hotspot Shield is adequate. For serious privacy protection — journalists, activists, whistleblowers — the closed-source Hydra protocol, US jurisdiction, lack of recurring audits, and the 2017 FTC complaint history make it a risky choice. NordVPN, ProtonVPN, or Mullvad are safer options for high-stakes privacy needs. ### What happened with the Hotspot Shield FTC complaint? In August 2017, the Center for Democracy and Technology (CDT) filed a formal complaint with the FTC alleging that AnchorFree (Hotspot Shield's then-parent company) engaged in deceptive trade practices. The allegations included sharing free-tier user data with advertisers, injecting JavaScript tracking code into web pages, and making misleading privacy claims. AnchorFree denied the JavaScript injection allegation. The FTC investigated but did not take formal enforcement action. The company has since been acquired twice (rebranded to Pango in 2019, then acquired by Aura in 2020) and updated its privacy policy, but the fundamental concerns about the free tier's ad-supported business model persist. ### What is the Hydra protocol and is it secure? Hydra (formerly Catapult Hydra) is Hotspot Shield's proprietary VPN protocol. It is built on TLS 1.2/1.3 with AES-256-GCM encryption and RSA-2048 authentication. In our speed tests, it consistently outperformed WireGuard by 15-53%. The protocol has been evaluated by AV-TEST, a German security institute, which gave it favorable ratings. However, Hydra is closed-source — its code is not publicly available for independent security researchers to audit. This is a significant limitation. You are trusting Aura's claims about the protocol's security without the ability to verify them, unlike open-source alternatives like WireGuard or OpenVPN. ### Is the Hotspot Shield free plan worth using? For very light, occasional use — checking email on airport Wi-Fi, for example — the free plan provides basic AES-256 encryption at no cost. However, the severe limitations make it barely functional: 500MB per day, a 2 Mbps speed cap (slower than most 4G connections), only 1 US server location, 1 device only, and ad-supported (meaning some anonymized data is shared with advertisers). ProtonVPN offers a dramatically better free tier with unlimited data, no speed cap, and servers in 5 countries. We recommend ProtonVPN Free over Hotspot Shield Free in almost every scenario. ### Who owns Hotspot Shield? Hotspot Shield is owned by Aura, a US-based digital security company. The ownership chain: AnchorFree (founded 2005 in Redwood City, California by David Gorodyansky and Eugene Malobrodsky) created Hotspot Shield in 2008. AnchorFree rebranded to Pango in 2019 and consolidated several VPN brands (Hotspot Shield, Betternet, VPN 360, HexaTech, TouchVPN). Aura acquired Pango in 2020, bringing all these VPN products under its umbrella alongside identity theft protection, credit monitoring, and antivirus services. Aura is headquartered in the United States, placing Hotspot Shield firmly under US (Five Eyes) jurisdiction. ### Does Hotspot Shield work with Netflix? Yes. In our March 2026 testing, Hotspot Shield reliably unblocked Netflix US (New York and Los Angeles servers), Netflix UK (London), and Netflix Japan (Tokyo). Streaming quality was excellent — 4K HDR content played without buffering on all tested servers thanks to Hydra's fast speeds. Hotspot Shield also includes dedicated streaming-optimized servers. The only major streaming platform that blocked Hotspot Shield in our tests was Hulu.

How Hotspot Shield Compares
